revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine
| name = Ogasa Shrine
| native_name = 小笠神社
| native_name_lang = ja
| image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}}
| map_type = Japan Shizuoka
| coordinates = {{coord|34|44|11|N|138|0|46|E|display=inline,title}}
| location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
| deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}}
| founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2)
| festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November
}}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities:
* '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation.
* '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto.
* '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends'''
The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:'''
852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:'''
- Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山)
- Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' ==
* [[Kumano Shrines]]
* [[Shinto in Japan]]
* [[Tengu Legends in Japan]]
* [[Emperor Monmu]]
== '''References''' ==
{{Reflist}}
== '''External Links''' ==
* [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association]
* [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism]
* [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{Japanese history-stub}}

revision.text:
{{神社 | 名称 = 小笠神社 | 画像 = {{画像募集中|cat=掛川市}} | 所在地 = [[静岡県]][[掛川市]][[入山瀬 (掛川市)|入山瀬]]852番地 | 位置 = {{Coord|34|44|11|N|138|0|46|E|display=inline,title}} | 祭神 = [[事解男尊]]
[[イザナミ|伊弉册尊]]
[[熊野速玉男神|速玉男尊]] | 神体 = | 社格 = 旧[[県社]] | 創建 = [[702年]]
（旧暦[[大宝 (日本)|大宝]]2年3月18日） | 本殿 = | 別名 = | 札所等 = | 例祭 = | 神事 = 矢矧祭 | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=小笠神社|coordinates= {{coord2|34|44|11|N|138|0|46|E}}|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|44|11|N|138|0|46|E}}}} }} '''小笠神社'''（おがさじんじゃ、{{Lang-en|Ogasa Jinja}}）は、[[静岡県]][[掛川市]]の[[神社]]である。[[近代社格制度]]における[[社格]]は[[県社]]。 == 概要 == 小笠神社は[[静岡県]][[掛川市]][[入山瀬 (掛川市)|入山瀬]]に鎮座する[[神社]]である。[[小笠山]]の山頂付近に立地している。[[祭神]]は事解男尊、[[イザナミ|伊弉册尊]]、[[熊野速玉男神|速玉男尊]]の3柱であり、[[熊野三山]]から勧請されたとされている。同時に創建された掛川市の[[三熊野神社 (掛川市)|三熊野神社]]、[[御前崎市]]の[[高松神社]]とともに「[[遠州の熊野三山]]」とも称される。 == 祭神 == * [[事解男尊]] * [[イザナミ|伊弉册尊]] * [[熊野速玉男神|速玉男尊]] == 歴史 == [[ファイル:Ogasa no Fue cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 44.jpg|thumb|right|200px|多聞天の伝承を描いた江戸時代の彩色画（「小笠の笛」[[藤長庚]]編集『[[遠江古蹟圖會]]』1803年。[[国立国会図書館]]蔵）。小笠神社で少年（左）が笛を吹いていると、天狗の三広坊（右）が現れる。三広坊に勧められ、少年は天狗の修行を積むことになり、やがて多聞天となった]] [[文武天皇]]の命により702年（旧暦[[大宝 (日本)|大宝]]2年3月18日）に創建されたとされる[[掛川市]]『掛川市歴史的風致維持向上計画』2018年1月、138頁。。1682年（旧暦[[天和 (日本)|天和]]2年）には[[西尾忠成]]により[[横須賀藩]]の[[祈願所]]に定められた。また、[[文化 (元号)|文化]]年間には[[太田資順]]により[[掛川藩]]の祈願所に定められた。 [[明治]]に入ると[[近代社格制度]]が導入され、小笠神社は[[村社]]の一つとして列せられた鈴木彌一郞編輯『現行神社法規』鈴木彌一郞、1908年、154頁。。1919年（[[大正]]8年）に[[郷社]]に昇格し、1945年（[[昭和]]20年）には[[県社]]に昇格した。 == 境内 == [[ファイル:Tengu Shuseki cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 45.jpg|thumb|200px|多聞天の伝承を描いた江戸時代の彩色画（「天狗手跡」[[藤長庚]]編集『[[遠江古蹟圖會]]』1803年。[[国立国会図書館]]蔵）。天狗の多聞天（奥）が[[遠江国]][[山名郡]]柴村の浄瑠璃寺を訪れ書を認めている]] 社殿は本殿、幣殿、拝殿から構成されており、拝殿の前に神楽殿がある。本殿は文化年間に建てられたとみなされている。 小笠神社には多聞天神社が隣接しており、小笠神社の祭礼の際には多聞天神社に矢が奉納されるなど、関係が深い神社の一つである[[掛川市役所|掛川市役所商工労働観光課]]『[http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp 小笠神社・矢矧祭（やはぎさい）]』[[掛川市役所]]、2007年9月21日。。多聞天神社が現在の地に創建された理由について、伝承によれば、小笠山に[[天狗]]が住んでおり、近隣の住民が天狗を[[毘沙門天|多聞天]]として多聞天神社に祀ったからとされている[[掛川市教育委員会|掛川市教育委員会生涯教育課文化振興係]]『[http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/bunka/culture_history/kyododensetsu/tamontensama.jsp 多聞天さま]』[[掛川市役所]]。。多聞天神社のほかには金比羅神社も鎮座している。 == 祭事・年中行事 == 11月には「矢矧祭」と呼ばれる[[神事]]が行われている。五穀豊穣、家内安全、安産を祈願する神事であり、神社の建立以来続く祭礼とされている。[[神職]]が放つ[[破魔矢]]を[[氏子]]が奪い合い、その矢を近隣の多聞天神社に奉納する神事である。 == 文化財 == 参道には樹高が20メートルほどの[[スギ]]があり、樹齢が400年以上と推定されることから、1990年（[[平成]]2年）に[[小笠郡]][[大東町 (静岡県)|大東町]]により[[天然記念物]]として文化財指定されている[[掛川市教育委員会|掛川市教育委員会生涯教育課文化振興室文化財係]]『[http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp 市天然記念物]』[[掛川市役所]]、2007年4月1日。[[掛川市教育委員会|掛川市教育委員会生涯教育課文化振興室文化財係]]『[http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/siteiitirannhyio.jsp 指定文化財一覧表]』[[掛川市役所]]、2007年4月1日。。 また、境内には[[小笠山砦]]の遺構も遺されている[[掛川市]]『掛川市歴史的風致維持向上計画』2018年1月、136頁。。 == 氏子区域 == * [[静岡県]][[掛川市]][[入山瀬 (掛川市)|入山瀬]] == 略歴 == * [[大宝 (日本)|大宝]]2年（[[702年]]） - 創建。 * [[天和 (日本)|天和]]2年（[[1682年]]） - [[横須賀藩]]の祈願所に指定。 * [[文化 (元号)|文化]]年間 - [[掛川藩]]の祈願所に指定。 * [[大正]]8年（[[1919年]]） - [[郷社]]に昇格。 * [[昭和]]20年（[[1945年]]） - [[県社]]に昇格。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 関連人物 == * [[文武天皇]] == 関連項目 == * [[熊野権現]] * [[熊野三山]] * [[熊野那智大社]] * [[天狗]] * [[小笠山]] * [[小笠山砦]] * [[入山瀬 (掛川市)]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 小笠神社] - 静岡県神社庁 * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html 小笠山] - 掛川市 * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf 掛川市歴史的風致維持向上計画 第2章(p.138)] - 掛川市 {{Shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{デフォルトソート:おかさしんしや}} [[Category:掛川市の神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧県社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:文武天皇]]

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|44|11|N|138|0|46|E}}}} }} '''小笠神社'''（おがさじんじゃ、{{Lang-en|Ogasa Jinja}}）は、[[静岡県]][[掛川市]]の[[神社]]である。[[近代社格制度]]における[[社格]]は[[県社]]。 == 概要 == 小笠神社は[[静岡県]][[掛川市]][[入山瀬 (掛川市)|入山瀬]]に鎮座する[[神社]]である。[[小笠山]]の山頂付近に立地している。[[祭神]]は事解男尊、[[イザナミ|伊弉册尊]]、[[熊野速玉男神|速玉男尊]]の3柱であり、[[熊野三山]]から勧請されたとされている。同時に創建された掛川市の[[三熊野神社 (掛川市)|三熊野神社]]、[[御前崎市]]の[[高松神社]]とともに「[[遠州の熊野三山]]」とも称される。 == 祭神 == * [[事解男尊]] * [[イザナミ|伊弉册尊]] * [[熊野速玉男神|速玉男尊]] == 歴史 == [[ファイル:Ogasa no Fue cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 44.jpg|thumb|right|200px|多聞天の伝承を描いた江戸時代の彩色画（「小笠の笛」[[藤長庚]]編集『[[遠江古蹟圖會]]』1803年。[[国立国会図書館]]蔵）。小笠神社で少年（左）が笛を吹いていると、天狗の三広坊（右）が現れる。三広坊に勧められ、少年は天狗の修行を積むことになり、やがて多聞天となった]] [[文武天皇]]の命により702年（旧暦[[大宝 (日本)|大宝]]2年3月18日）に創建されたとされる[[掛川市]]『掛川市歴史的風致維持向上計画』2018年1月、138頁。。1682年（旧暦[[天和 (日本)|天和]]2年）には[[西尾忠成]]により[[横須賀藩]]の[[祈願所]]に定められた。また、[[文化 (元号)|文化]]年間には[[太田資順]]により[[掛川藩]]の祈願所に定められた。 [[明治]]に入ると[[近代社格制度]]が導入され、小笠神社は[[村社]]の一つとして列せられた鈴木彌一郞編輯『現行神社法規』鈴木彌一郞、1908年、154頁。。1919年（[[大正]]8年）に[[郷社]]に昇格し、1945年（[[昭和]]20年）には[[県社]]に昇格した。 == 境内 == [[ファイル:Tengu Shuseki cropped Totomi Koseki Zue Frame 45.jpg|thumb|200px|多聞天の伝承を描いた江戸時代の彩色画（「天狗手跡」[[藤長庚]]編集『[[遠江古蹟圖會]]』1803年。[[国立国会図書館]]蔵）。天狗の多聞天（奥）が[[遠江国]][[山名郡]]柴村の浄瑠璃寺を訪れ書を認めている]] 社殿は本殿、幣殿、拝殿から構成されており、拝殿の前に神楽殿がある。本殿は文化年間に建てられたとみなされている。 小笠神社には多聞天神社が隣接しており、小笠神社の祭礼の際には多聞天神社に矢が奉納されるなど、関係が深い神社の一つである[[掛川市役所|掛川市役所商工労働観光課]]『[http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp 小笠神社・矢矧祭（やはぎさい）]』[[掛川市役所]]、2007年9月21日。。多聞天神社が現在の地に創建された理由について、伝承によれば、小笠山に[[天狗]]が住んでおり、近隣の住民が天狗を[[毘沙門天|多聞天]]として多聞天神社に祀ったからとされている[[掛川市教育委員会|掛川市教育委員会生涯教育課文化振興係]]『[http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/bunka/culture_history/kyododensetsu/tamontensama.jsp 多聞天さま]』[[掛川市役所]]。。多聞天神社のほかには金比羅神社も鎮座している。 == 祭事・年中行事 == 11月には「矢矧祭」と呼ばれる[[神事]]が行われている。五穀豊穣、家内安全、安産を祈願する神事であり、神社の建立以来続く祭礼とされている。[[神職]]が放つ[[破魔矢]]を[[氏子]]が奪い合い、その矢を近隣の多聞天神社に奉納する神事である。 == 文化財 == 参道には樹高が20メートルほどの[[スギ]]があり、樹齢が400年以上と推定されることから、1990年（[[平成]]2年）に[[小笠郡]][[大東町 (静岡県)|大東町]]により[[天然記念物]]として文化財指定されている[[掛川市教育委員会|掛川市教育委員会生涯教育課文化振興室文化財係]]『[http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp 市天然記念物]』[[掛川市役所]]、2007年4月1日。[[掛川市教育委員会|掛川市教育委員会生涯教育課文化振興室文化財係]]『[http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/siteiitirannhyio.jsp 指定文化財一覧表]』[[掛川市役所]]、2007年4月1日。。 また、境内には[[小笠山砦]]の遺構も遺されている[[掛川市]]『掛川市歴史的風致維持向上計画』2018年1月、136頁。。 == 氏子区域 == * [[静岡県]][[掛川市]][[入山瀬 (掛川市)|入山瀬]] == 略歴 == * [[大宝 (日本)|大宝]]2年（[[702年]]） - 創建。 * [[天和 (日本)|天和]]2年（[[1682年]]） - [[横須賀藩]]の祈願所に指定。 * [[文化 (元号)|文化]]年間 - [[掛川藩]]の祈願所に指定。 * [[大正]]8年（[[1919年]]） - [[郷社]]に昇格。 * [[昭和]]20年（[[1945年]]） - [[県社]]に昇格。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 関連人物 == * [[文武天皇]] == 関連項目 == * [[熊野権現]] * [[熊野三山]] * [[熊野那智大社]] * [[天狗]] * [[小笠山]] * [[小笠山砦]] * [[入山瀬 (掛川市)]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 小笠神社] - 静岡県神社庁 * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html 小笠山] - 掛川市 * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf 掛川市歴史的風致維持向上計画 第2章(p.138)] - 掛川市 {{Shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{デフォルトソート:おかさしんしや}} [[Category:掛川市の神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧県社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:文武天皇]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{coord|34|44|11|N|138|0|46|E|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[Emperor Monmu]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{coord|34|44|11|N|138|0|46|E|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[Emperor Monmu]]
== '''References''' == {{Reflist}}
== '''External Links''' == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:文武天皇
revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{coord|34|44|11|N|138|0|46|E|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[Emperor Monmu]]
== '''References''' == {{Reflist}}
== '''External Links''' == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]

'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[Emperor Monmu]]

'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]
== '''References''' == {{Reflist}}
== '''External Links''' == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{coord|34|44|11|N|138|0|46|E|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]
== '''References''' == {{Reflist}}
== '''External Links''' == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{coord|34|44|11|N|138|0|46|E|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]
== '''References''' == {{Reflist}}
== '''External Links''' == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{Japanese history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|小笠神社|version=103367052|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11463433]] Wikidata name: Ogasa Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{coord|34|44|11|N|138|0|46|E|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]
== '''References''' == {{Reflist}}
== '''External Links''' == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]
{{Shinto-stub}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11463433]] Wikidata name: Ogasa Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73621389650273|N|138.01279363214334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November | religious_affiliation= {{ill|Shinto|qid=Q812767}} | prefecture= {{ill|Kakegawa|qid=Q823988}} | founded_by= {{ill|Monmu|qid=Q357246}} }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]
== '''References''' == {{Reflist}}
== '''External Links''' == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]

== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ogasa Jinja]][[ja:小笠神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11463433]] Wikidata name: Ogasa Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73621389650273|N|138.01279363214334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November | religious_affiliation= {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} | prefecture= {{ill|Kakegawa|WD=Q823988}} | founded_by= {{ill|Monmu|WD=Q357246}} }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]

'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ogasa Jinja]][[ja:小笠神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11463433]] Wikidata name: Ogasa Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73621389650273|N|138.01279363214334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November | religious_affiliation= {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} | prefecture= {{ill|Kakegawa|WD=Q823988}} | founded_by= {{ill|Monmu|WD=Q357246}} }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]
== '''References''' == {{Reflist}}
== '''External Links''' == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]

During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>

== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Ogasa Jinja]][[ja:小笠神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Ogasa Jinja]][[ja:小笠神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ogasa Jinja]][[ja:小笠神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11463433]] Wikidata name: Ogasa Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73621389650273|N|138.01279363214334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November | religious_affiliation= {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} | prefecture= {{ill|Kakegawa|WD=Q823988}} | founded_by= {{ill|Monmu|WD=Q357246}} }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]
== '''References''' == {{Reflist}}
