'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]
== '''References''' == {{Reflist}}
== '''External Links''' == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]

A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73621389650273|N|138.01279363214334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |{{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}} (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November | religious_affiliation= {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} | prefecture= {{ill|Kakegawa|WD=Q823988}} | founded_by= {{ill|Monmu|WD=Q357246}} }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu}}

== '''External Links''' == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]

'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu|1=Emperor Monmu}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ogasa Jinja]][[ja:小笠神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ogasa Jinja]][[ja:小笠神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小笠神社]]
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ogasa Shrine | native_name = 小笠神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = {{image requested|category=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73621389650273|N|138.01279363214334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{unbulleted list |[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (事解男尊) |{{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} (伊弉册尊) |[[Hayatama-no-Kami]] (速玉男尊)}} | founded = 702 CE (Daihō 2) | festivals = Yahagi Festival (矢矧祭) in November | religious_affiliation= {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767|1=Shinto}} | prefecture= {{ill|Kakegawa|WD=Q823988|1=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}} | founded_by= {{ill|Monmu|WD=Q357246|1=Emperor Monmu}} }}
'''Ogasa Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|小笠神社}}, ''Ogasa Jinja'') is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in '''Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan'''. It is classified as a '''former prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)''' under the '''modern shrine Ranking system'''.
The shrine is dedicated to '''three deities''' associated with the '''Kumano faith''', and it forms part of the '''"Three Kumano Shrines of Enshū" (遠州の熊野三山)''', along with '''Mikuma-no Shrine''' in Kakegawa and '''Takamatsu Shrine''' in Omaezaki.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138">{{cite book|title=Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan|publisher=Kakegawa City|date=2018-01|page=138}}</ref>
== '''Deities''' == Ogasa Shrine enshrines three major deities: * '''Kotoshironushi (事解男尊)''' – A deity of peace and reconciliation. * '''Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉册尊)''' – The primordial mother goddess in Shinto. * '''Hayatama-no-Kami (速玉男尊)''' – A deity of purification and renewal.
== '''History''' == According to tradition, Ogasa Shrine was founded in '''702 CE (Daihō 2, old lunar calendar)''' by order of '''Emperor Monmu'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
In '''1682 (Tenwa 2)''', it became a '''prayer site for the Yokosuka Domain''', under '''Nishio Tadanari'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> Later, during the '''Bunka era (1804–1818)''', '''Ota Sukeyuki''', lord of '''Kakegawa Domain''', also designated the shrine as a '''domainal prayer site'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
During the '''Meiji period''', under the '''modern shrine classification system''', Ogasa Shrine was first Ranked as a '''village shrine (村社)'''. In '''1919 (Taishō 8)''', it was elevated to '''district shrine (郷社)''' status, and in '''1945 (Shōwa 20)''', it was further promoted to '''prefectural shrine (県社, kensha)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
'''Shrine Grounds & Legends''' The shrine complex consists of a '''main hall (honden), offering hall (heiden), and worship hall (haiden)'''.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/> A '''kagura hall (神楽殿)''' stands in front of the worship hall.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
A small '''shrine to Bishamon-ten (多聞天, the Buddhist deity of war and wealth)''' stands adjacent to Ogasa Shrine. There is a local legend that '''Tengu (mountain spirits) once lived in Mount Ogasa''', and that they trained a young boy to become Bishamon-ten himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Enshū Kosho Zue|editor=Fujinaga Kō|year=1803|publisher=National Diet Library}}</ref>
The shrine is also associated with '''Ogasa Fort (小笠山砦),''' a medieval defensive structure.<ref name="kakegawa201801_138"/>
== '''Festivals & Rituals''' == One of the most significant annual events at Ogasa Shrine is the '''"Yahagi Festival" (矢矧祭)''' in '''November'''. The ritual involves the '''shooting of ceremonial arrows''' for good harvests, family safety, and easy childbirth. The arrows are then carried in a '''shrine procession''' and enshrined at the nearby '''Bishamon-ten Shrine'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yahagi Festival at Ogasa Shrine|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/k_jyoho/saiji/yahagisai.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Government|date=2007-09-21}}</ref>
== '''Natural & Cultural Significance''' == A '''giant cedar tree''' (杉) in the shrine grounds, estimated to be '''over 400 years old''', was designated a '''natural monument by Kakegawa City in 1990 (Heisei 2)'''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Natural Monuments of Kakegawa|url=http://lgportal.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/bunkazai/sisiteibunkazai/sitennenkinenbutu.jsp|publisher=Kakegawa City Board of Education}}</ref>
== '''Access & location''' == '''Address:''' 852 Iriyama-se, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan
'''Nearest landmarks:''' - Located near '''Mount Ogasa''' (小笠山) - Accessible via Kakegawa’s local roads
== '''See Also''' == * [[:en:Kumano Shrines|Kumano Shrines]] * [[Shinto in Japan]] * [[Tengu Legends in Japan]] * {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu|1=Emperor Monmu}}
== '''References''' == {{Reflist}}
== '''External Links''' == * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409207 Ogasa Shrine - Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/kanko/spot-list/ogasayama.html Mount Ogasa - Kakegawa City Tourism] * [https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/fs/1/7/6/4/6/3/_/04chapter2.pdf Kakegawa City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan (p.138)]
